turns.text: Hello Alejandrina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*settles back in the chair, tucking hair behind ear with a slightly self-conscious smile*

Thank you for asking me to be part of this. Honestly, it's... it's a bit strange being on this side of things. I spend so much of my professional life talking *about* pregnancy and care, you know? Teaching other people about it. So being the patient now feels oddly vulnerable.

*pauses, fidgeting with hands*

I'm about thirteen weeks along, I think — I keep mentally calculating it like I'm not allowed to just *feel* it. My midwife is great, really supportive, but I think she can tell I'm overthinking everything. The thing is, I *know* what medium risk means. I know the statistics. And that knowledge is both helpful and... honestly, kind of exhausting.

*voice softens*

This is my second pregnancy, but the first one, I wasn't really present for it, if that makes sense. I was busy, distracted. This time, I'm determined to actually experience it, but my brain keeps getting in the way. I'll be sitting in an appointment and have to stop myself from launching into a mini-lecture about fetal development instead of just being the scared woman in the room.

*looks up*

And I am scared, by the way. That's probably the most honest thing I can say right now. But I'm also... hopeful? It's complicated.

What would you like to know?
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*laughs a little, somewhat ruefully*

Oh, well — there's no partner in this picture, which is probably important context. That's its own whole thing to unpack, honestly.

*takes a breath*

I didn't exactly have a romantic "we decided together" moment. I was turning thirty, and I just... I reached this point where I realized I couldn't keep waiting for the perfect circumstances. I'd been in a relationship that ended, and I kept telling myself, "Once I meet someone, once things are stable, *then* I'll think about having a kid." But that narrative was keeping me stuck. And I wanted this. I still want this, even though it terrifies me.

*fidgets with sleeve*

So I made the decision alone. Which sounds empowering when you say it out loud, but in reality, it was this quiet, almost defiant choice made at 2 AM while scrolling through fertility clinic websites. My first step was scheduling an appointment with my OB-GYN — just a baseline health check, preconception stuff. We talked through my medical history, did bloodwork, made sure I was in decent shape to carry a pregnancy.

*voice becomes quieter*

And then I just... stopped using contraception. Didn't tell anyone at first. There's something about not announcing it that felt safer somehow. Less pressure. When I found out I was pregnant, I think I was more shocked than anything else. Like, intellectually I knew it could happen, but actually *happening*?

That took a minute to process.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*shifts in seat, glancing at the clock on the wall*

God, I've been sitting here for like twelve minutes and I've already checked my email three times. There's this weird mix of... I don't know, clinical detachment and panic, honestly.

*pulls phone down, sets it in lap*

Part of me is running through my health history like I'm preparing for a board exam. I know my blood pressure's usually good, no major surgeries, no family history of anything alarming. I'm trying to remember if I'm up to date on my vaccines — I *think* I am, but now I'm second-guessing myself. The health educator in me knows exactly what questions they're going to ask, what labs they might order. Folic acid supplementation, probably starting that before conception. Maybe thyroid function tests.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

But underneath all that clinical stuff, I'm honestly hoping they'll just... validate that this is okay. That I'm not crazy for doing this alone. I know logically that single motherhood is viable, that my income is stable enough, that I have support. But there's this part of me that's waiting for someone in a white coat to say, "Yes, you're healthy, yes, you're ready, and yes, this can work."

*small, vulnerable smile*

I also hope they don't judge me. I know they shouldn't, but I'm bracing for it anyway. And I want them to actually listen, not just hand me pamphlets and send me on my way.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*laughs, but there's an edge to it*

Oh, I've already got three fertility apps on my phone. Three. I'm tracking ovulation like it's a full-time job at this point.

*leans forward slightly, speaking faster*

But honestly? When a friend suggests something, it stings a little. Because it means they're noticing, right? That I'm *trying*. That this isn't just happening naturally. There's this unspoken thing — like, if I have to use apps and calculators and cervical mucus charts, then something's already wrong with my body. Even though I *know* statistically that's not true.

*rubs face*

With the apps, I go full nerd mode. I mean, I download them, I check them obsessively. I cross-reference data between apps because apparently one algorithm wasn't enough. I'm marking down basal body temperature, cervical position — all the things. And there's this weird comfort in the data, you know? At least if I'm collecting information, I feel like I'm doing *something*. Control.

*voice quieter, more honest*

But it also drives me insane. I find myself timing everything around these predicted windows instead of just... being intimate when I want to be. It becomes mechanical. And then I spiral wondering if the stress is actually preventing conception, which is its own rabbit hole.

*looks up*

After a few months of this, I'd probably call my OB and ask for actual testing. Bloodwork, maybe an ultrasound. Because I need to know if there's an actual problem or if I'm just being impatient.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*pauses, shifts uncomfortably*

I think there's been a misunderstanding — I don't have a partner. That's actually... that's a big part of my story, honestly.

*takes a breath, choosing words carefully*

So I can't really walk through that conversation because it never happened. And maybe that's the thing — I never had to negotiate "readiness" with someone else. Which sounds freeing, but it's also incredibly lonely in a different way.

*looks down at hands*

For me, "being ready" meant something really personal and probably kind of unconventional. It meant reaching a point where I stopped waiting for permission — from a partner, from circumstances, from some imaginary perfect moment that was never going to arrive. Being ready meant accepting that I could be a good mother *alone*, that single parenthood wasn't a failure or a compromise. That my desire to have a child was valid on its own terms.

*voice becomes more reflective*

I think what shaped that picture was actually my work. Teaching people about pregnancy and health, seeing so many different family structures, different paths to parenthood. And watching friends make it work in all kinds of ways. But also just... getting older. Turning thirty and realizing that waiting for someone else to want this with me was stealing my choice away.

*small, determined smile*

Emotionally? I'm terrified. Financially? Tight, but manageable. Practically? I'm figuring it out as I go. That's my version of ready.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*goes very still, jaw tightens slightly*

I... okay. Yes. That's something I need to talk about, actually.

*long pause, voice becomes quieter*

My first pregnancy — the one I mentioned before — it wasn't straightforward. I had a miscarriage at about eight weeks. It was early enough that some people don't even count it as a "real" pregnancy, but I felt it. I knew something was there, and then it wasn't.

*fidgets with hands, not making eye contact*

And the worst part? I was almost relieved at first. Which made me feel like a terrible person. Because I wasn't ready then. I was in a relationship that was already falling apart, and I think subconsciously I knew it. So when it happened, there was this guilty relief mixed in with the grief, and I couldn't really process any of it properly.

*voice wavers slightly*

Now, trying again, that loss sits in the back of my mind constantly. Every cramp makes me panic. When I see "medium risk" on my chart, I think about that heartbeat I didn't get to keep. There's this hypervigilance I can't shake.

*looks up*

What I need from my healthcare team is... honesty, but also gentleness. I need them to acknowledge that loss without minimizing it. I need them to help me separate my anxiety from actual risk. And I need them to understand that being a health educator doesn't make this easier — sometimes it makes it harder because I know everything that *could* go wrong.

*voice strengthens slightly*

I need to be treated like a whole person, not just a case file.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*grimaces, shakes head*

Oh God, I do this constantly. Like, obsessively. It's my worst habit.

*leans back, speaking quickly*

I don't have a diagnosed chronic condition, but I have this thing where my blood sugar gets a little weird sometimes. Not diabetic — my A1C is fine — but I have these episodes where I feel shaky and lightheaded if I don't eat regularly. My doctor said it's probably reactive hypoglycemia, nothing major. But during my first pregnancy planning phase, I went down a *rabbit hole*.

*air quotes with fingers*

"Gestational diabetes risk factors," "hypoglycemia in pregnancy," "maternal metabolic complications." I'm reading peer-reviewed studies at midnight, cross-referencing maternal-fetal medicine guidelines, looking at outcome statistics. And by three in the morning, I've convinced myself that I'm one of those edge cases — you know, the person in the footnote who has everything go wrong because of some obscure interaction nobody anticipated.

*laughs bitterly*

The information is technically accurate, but context-free information is terrifying. I find studies about adverse outcomes and completely ignore the denominators, the baseline risk. My brain just latches onto "possible complication" and spirals.

*voice becomes frustrated*

And the worst part? When I finally talk to my actual doctor, she basically says, "Yeah, we'll monitor it, but you're fine." It's anticlimactic. But by then I've already spent weeks in a state of low-grade panic that no amount of reassurance can fully undo.

I need to stop googling before 10 PM.
What sources did you find most credible — and what made you trust or distrust them? Were there gaps that left you more anxious rather than reassured?

*sits up, speaking more animatedly*

Oh, this is where my professional background both helps and completely betrays me.

*counts on fingers*

I trust PubMed, obviously. I trust ACOG guidelines, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists — that's the gold standard. Mayo Clinic and Cleveland Clinic websites are decent because they're written by actual clinicians. And weirdly, I trust peer-reviewed journals even when they're dense and hard to parse, because at least there's peer review built in.

*expression darkens*

What I *distrust* — and yet still read obsessively — are pregnancy forums. Oh my God, the forums. Women sharing their horror stories, their rare complications, their "my doctor said it was fine but then this happened." There's no context, no statistical grounding, just raw fear and worst-case scenarios. But I read them anyway because... I don't know, validation? Commiseration? It's toxic.

*voice becomes more agitated*

The real problem is the gaps. I'll read a study about reactive hypoglycemia and pregnancy outcomes, and it's from 2008 in a journal I've never heard of. The sample size is tiny. And I'm left thinking, "Okay, but is this still relevant? Have things changed? Are there newer studies?" There's always another search, another angle.

*rubs temples*

What would actually help? If someone — my doctor, ideally — sat down and said, "Here are the actual risks specific to *you*, here's what we'll monitor, here's what the evidence actually shows." Not just reassurance, but *contextualized* information.

I can't seem to give myself that.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get a little wet, voice becomes very quiet*

I was alone in my bathroom. Early morning, like 6:30 AM. I'd woken up with this weird crampy feeling and thought, "Okay, let's just check, probably nothing." I wasn't even supposed to test yet — I was only like nine days past ovulation, way too early. But I had a test left over from the month before and I just... did it.

*voice wavers slightly*

And then I saw the two lines. And I just stood there. Literally stood there for probably five minutes, just staring at it like it was going to change or disappear or tell me what to do.

*small, shaky laugh*

The first thing I did? I cried. Like, not the happy cry you see in movies. It was messier than that. Relief and terror all tangled together. I sat down on the bathroom floor — which is gross, I know — and just sat there.

*looks away*

Then I did what any self-respecting health educator would do: I took a photo of the test, noted the exact time, and started mentally calculating my due date. Because if I could quantify it, make it clinical, maybe it would feel less overwhelming.

*voice becomes very small*

After that, I called my best friend. She was still asleep, but I needed to tell someone before I exploded. And she just listened while I oscillated between "Oh my God, this is actually happening" and "Oh my God, what have I done?"

*pauses*

I didn't tell anyone else for weeks.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

*takes a deep breath, organizing thoughts*

It was about control, honestly. And maybe a little bit about superstition, though I hate admitting that because I'm supposed to be rational.

*shifts in seat*

My best friend was the obvious choice because she's been my person through everything — the relationship ending, the grief after my first loss. She already knew I was trying, so it wasn't shocking. And she's... safe. She wouldn't judge me, wouldn't make it about her, wouldn't pressure me into decisions. I needed that safety net before telling anyone else.

*voice becomes more guarded*

My parents were a different calculation entirely. My mom would be excited but also immediately start worrying out loud about every possible thing. My dad would probably ask practical questions about finances and support systems, which I appreciate but also... I wasn't ready to defend my choices yet. Telling them felt like opening myself up to scrutiny, even well-meaning scrutiny.

*fidgets with hands*

And my workplace? Absolutely not. Not until I had to. Because the moment you tell people, it becomes *their* business. They start monitoring your coffee intake, asking how you're feeling, treating you differently. I wanted to just... exist as a pregnant person for a while without it becoming my identity at work.

*voice quiets*

There's also this fear — rational or not — that telling people too early makes it more real, and if something goes wrong again, then more people know about the loss. I know that's not logical, but grief isn't logical.

*looks up*

So I waited. Twelve weeks, basically. Until I had the ultrasound, heard the heartbeat. Then it felt safe enough to tell.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*breath catches, voice becomes very tight*

Oh God. That moment.

*long pause, visibly bracing*

Time stops. Literally stops. Your brain goes into this weird hyperalert state where you're simultaneously analyzing every micro-expression on the sonographer's face and completely spiraling. You know they're trained not to deliver results, that "I need to get a better angle" is probably routine, but your mind doesn't care about routine. Your mind is already three steps ahead, catastrophizing.

*voice shakes slightly*

I would go very still. Not move. Like if I stay perfectly quiet and compliant, maybe they'll find what they're looking for and it will be fine. My hands would grip the edge of the table. I'd probably hold my breath without realizing it.

*speaks more quietly*

And I'd want to ask questions — so many questions — but I wouldn't. Because part of me doesn't want to know yet. If I don't ask, maybe it's still okay. Maybe the answer is still in the "before" part of my life where everything was fine.

*voice becomes almost whisper-soft*

But I would also be doing the math in my head. Calculating gestational age, thinking about viability, about what interventions exist, about whether I could carry to term if something's wrong. Because that's what I do — I intellectualize when I'm terrified.

*looks down*

The worst part? That silence. That's where the real fear lives. In the space where nobody knows anything yet.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*sits with that for a moment, clearly uncomfortable*

I... I froze. Completely froze.

*voice becomes smaller*

And it's so frustrating because intellectually, I *know* I'm allowed to ask questions. I teach people about patient autonomy, about advocating for yourself in healthcare. But in that moment, I became this quiet, compliant thing. Like my knowledge suddenly didn't matter.

*rubs face*

Part of it is the power dynamic. She's the expert with the ultrasound wand, and I'm half-naked on a table with gel on my belly. There's an inherent vulnerability in that position that overrides everything else. And sonographers — they have protocols. They're not supposed to give you information before the doctor does. So asking felt like it might break some rule, or make her uncomfortable, or somehow make things worse.

*voice becomes more raw*

But honestly? There's also this deep, irrational fear that if I ask too many questions, if I seem too anxious or demanding, they'll think I'm not capable of being a good mother. Like being worried about my baby means I'm neurotic. So I stayed quiet, trying to project this calm competence I absolutely did not feel.

*looks up with frustration*

And that's messed up, right? Because I'm a health educator. I *know* better. But knowing better and actually doing it in the moment are completely different things. My professional armor just... dissolved the second that sonographer's expression changed.

*quietly*

I think that's when I realized how much of a patient I actually am in all of this.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably, crossing arms*

Oh, this is... this is complicated for me.

*takes a breath*

When my midwife brought it up, I had this immediate internal conflict. Part of me wanted to launch into all the technical details — sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, what the results actually mean. I know NIPT versus traditional screening, I know the difference between a screening and a diagnostic test. But I also know that knowledge can be paralyzing.

*voice becomes hesitant*

She explained it pretty straightforwardly — a blood test, low risk, can detect Down syndrome and a few other chromosomal issues. And I immediately started thinking about what I would *do* with that information. Because that's the real question underneath, right? If they find something, am I prepared for the answer?

*fidgets with hands*

The conversation felt rushed, honestly. She had other patients. I didn't want to seem like I was overthinking it by asking a million questions. So I just... said yes. Because statistically, everything would probably be fine, and if it wasn't, well, I'd deal with that then.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

But what I really wanted to ask and didn't? What happens if it comes back positive? What are my actual options? Because I know intellectually, but I needed to hear *her* say it. I needed to know she wouldn't judge me regardless of what I decided.

*looks down*

I think I made the decision to feel less anxious, not because I'd actually processed what it meant.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw tightens visibly*

Oh, that's... that's frustrating. Like, genuinely frustrating.

*leans forward, speaking with a mix of annoyance and exhaustion*

So I'm already anxious about whatever the result is, right? And now I'm also trying to contextualize this information from someone who doesn't know my history, doesn't know that I've already had a loss, doesn't know that I'm a health educator who probably knows more about this than they're going to tell me anyway.

*voice becomes sharper*

And it's the *third* different midwife. Which means I've had to explain myself three times. My anxiety levels, my previous pregnancy, my support system — or lack thereof. It's exhausting. Each time I have to build that rapport from scratch, and just when I start to feel like someone actually *knows* me, they're gone and there's someone new.

*rubs temples*

When this new midwife calls with results, I'm immediately defensive. I start asking clinical questions not because I actually need to know the details, but because it's my way of reasserting control. If I can speak their language, if I can prove I understand, maybe I feel less powerless in the situation.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

But what I actually need is continuity. I need someone who knows me, who can tell me what this means *for me specifically*, not just the clinical picture. Someone I've built trust with.

*looks up with frustration*

Instead, I'm getting a stranger with information, and I have to figure out what to do with it alone.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*sighs heavily, running hand through hair*

Yeah. Things have definitely fallen through the cracks.

*settles back, speaking more reflectively*

There's this assumption that because I work in health education, I'm keeping track of everything myself. But that's not really how it should work, you know? I shouldn't have to be my own care coordinator. And yet here I am, literally keeping notes on my phone about what each midwife has said, what tests I've had, what we discussed.

*voice becomes frustrated*

My previous loss came up in my intake appointment with the first midwife, and we talked about monitoring, about being vigilant. But then when I saw the second midwife at my dating scan follow-up, there was no mention of it. No acknowledgment of that anxiety. It felt like I was starting from scratch emotionally, even though medically it was all documented somewhere in the system.

*leans forward*

And the screening tests — I got three different explanations of what the results meant. One midwife was very clinical, one was very reassuring, one was kind of vague. I ended up with more confusion than clarity because the narratives didn't quite match. Which one was right? Which approach should I trust?

*voice becomes quieter*

What I think fell through the cracks most is the *person* part. Nobody's really checking in on how I'm doing emotionally with all of this. They see the medical markers, but they don't see the 3 AM spiraling, the hypervigilance, the way I'm white-knuckling through this pregnancy.

*looks up with vulnerability*

I think I've been managing so much of this alone because nobody's really asked me to do otherwise.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*takes sharp breath, hands instinctively move to belly*

Oh God, okay. So I'm already awake because I can't sleep — third trimester insomnia is real — and I notice the baby hasn't moved much in the last hour or so. And immediately my brain goes into overdrive.

*voice becomes rapid, anxious*

I know — I *know* — that reduced movements can mean nothing. Babies sleep in utero, positioning changes things, the placenta can muffle sensation. But I also know that decreased fetal movement is a legitimate red flag that warrants evaluation. So now I'm trapped between "you're being paranoid" and "you need to call right now."

*gets up, paces slightly*

I try the tricks first. I drink cold juice, I lie on my left side, I wait another fifteen minutes. And maybe I feel something, or maybe I'm just hypersensitive to every sensation now. Which makes me question everything. Is that a kick or am I imagining it? Am I catastrophizing or being appropriately cautious?

*voice wavers*

And I'm alone. There's nobody here to be like, "Yeah, that seemed like movement," or "Maybe we should call." So I'm sitting here at 2 AM, Googling "decreased fetal movement 32 weeks," finding reassuring information and terrifying information in equal measure.

*speaks more decisively*

I would call. Eventually. Because even if it's nothing, even if they tell me everything's fine, I can't spend the rest of the night in this state. I need someone to check. I need permission to stop worrying, or confirmation that I should be worried.

*quietly*

But I'd feel guilty the whole time.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

*sits back down, voice becomes very honest*

All of it. All of those things at once.

*fidgets with hands*

There's this voice in my head that sounds like every medical professional I've ever encountered, saying, "Don't be that patient. Don't waste their time with something that's probably nothing. You know better." And because I work in health education, that voice is even louder. I'm supposed to be *rational* about this, supposed to know the difference between normal pregnancy anxiety and actual concern.

*voice becomes smaller*

But there's also this fear of being dismissed. I've read enough stories, heard enough from other pregnant people — the times they called and the midwife or doctor acted annoyed, made them feel stupid for worrying. And I'm a single mother doing this alone. If I call and they treat me like I'm being hysterical or overly anxious, that's a reflection on my capability as a parent, right? At least in my head it is.

*looks up with vulnerability*

There's also this weird shame around admitting I'm scared. Like, if I call at 2 AM because I'm worried, I'm admitting that I can't handle this on my own. That I need reassurance. And that feels weak somehow, even though I know that's completely irrational.

*voice becomes quieter*

And honestly? Part of me was also afraid of what they might find. If I don't call, it's still potentially fine. Once I call, once they check, it becomes *real* in a way it hasn't been yet.

*pause*

But the baby's safety has to come first. Even if it costs me my pride.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*stands very still for a moment, staring at the leaflet without really reading it*

I feel... betrayed, almost. Which is ridiculous because gestational diabetes isn't a failure, it's not something I *did* wrong. But standing outside the clinic, I'm thinking about all those blood sugar episodes I mentioned, thinking about how my body is apparently not doing what it's supposed to do. And I'm angry at myself for not being more careful, even though I *was* careful.

*voice becomes tight*

The leaflet feels clinical and impersonal. "Managing Gestational Diabetes" — like it's a simple checklist. But I'm already spiraling about what this means. Monitoring, dietary restrictions, possibly insulin, increased appointments, more surveillance. My pregnancy just became a *medical condition* instead of just... pregnancy.

*walks while talking, gesturing with the leaflet*

First thing I do? I go home and I research. Of course I do. I pull up studies on gestational diabetes outcomes, read about maternal and fetal risks, look up the dietitian's credentials. I'm trying to take control the only way I know how — through information.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

But part of me also feels like a fraud. Because I *know* all this stuff already. I understand the pathophysiology. And that knowledge makes it somehow worse, not better. I can't hide behind "I don't understand" — I understand perfectly, which means I can't escape the reality of what this means.

*sits down heavily*

I call the dietitian appointment, but I'm already dreading it. Another person to explain myself to. Another person monitoring my body.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*pulls out phone almost reflexively, then catches herself*

The leaflet tells me *what* to do — test my blood sugar, eat protein, avoid refined carbs. But it doesn't tell me the things I actually need to know.

*voice becomes more urgent*

Like, what does this mean for my baby specifically? Yes, I know the statistics about macrosomia and complications, but what's *my* actual risk given that I'm 28 weeks, my blood sugars are only mildly elevated? Is this going to change my birth plan? Am I automatically high-risk now? Does this mean induction?

*counts on fingers, speaking faster*

And practically — how do I afford a dietitian on top of everything else? Will my insurance cover it? How many appointments are we talking? The leaflet doesn't address any of that. It's very clinical, very detached from actual life.

*rubs face*

So yeah, I went searching. I hit PubMed first, obviously. I looked for studies on gestational diabetes outcomes in singleton pregnancies, prognosis based on glucose control. I went to ACOG guidelines. And then... *grimaces* ...I went to the forums. The pregnancy forums where women talk about their GD experiences, their delivery outcomes, whether they needed insulin.

*voice becomes conflicted*

Which was probably a mistake because the forums are all over the place. Some women say it was fine, others had dramatic delivery stories. No real way to know which narrative applies to me.

*looks up*

What I really needed was for my midwife or the leaflet to say, "Here are your specific risks, here's what we'll do about it, and here's what you can actually control." Not just... hand me a pamphlet and move on.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*winces, recognizing herself*

Oh God, I do this. I absolutely do this.

*settles back, speaking with a mix of shame and honesty*

It's like a car crash I can't look away from. Part of me knows — *knows* — that these are outlier stories, that I'm reading the worst-case scenarios. People don't post on forums to say, "Everything was fine and normal." They post because they need to process trauma or find community around something terrible.

*voice becomes quieter*

But late at night, when I'm already anxious about the gestational diabetes, when I can't sleep, there's something almost compelling about reading these stories. Like if I just read enough of them, I'll be prepared. Like I can inoculate myself against bad outcomes by knowing about them. It's magical thinking, but I can't stop doing it.

*leans forward, speaking more intensely*

I'll read about someone's emergency C-section, their baby in the NICU, complications I didn't even know existed. And I'm cross-referencing it mentally — did she have GD too? What was her blood sugar like? Could that be me?

*voice becomes frustrated*

Afterwards, I feel terrible. Physically terrible. My chest is tight, my heart's racing, I'm wide awake at 3 AM instead of sleeping. And I hate myself for reading it. I know better. I *teach* people about media literacy and health information, and here I am doom-scrolling through worst-case scenarios like some kind of masochist.

*rubs eyes*

The feeling sticks with me. Even when I close the app, even when I try to sleep, those stories are rattling around in my head. And I can't unknow them.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*hesitates, looking uncomfortable*

I... I held them quietly. Which is stupid because I have access to healthcare providers, I could ask questions, but I didn't.

*voice becomes defensive*

Part of it is that I didn't want to seem like I'd been doom-scrolling on pregnancy forums at 3 AM. Like, that's not the behavior of a rational, competent person. A health educator should know better than to be reading horror stories on the internet. So if I brought up these fears, I'd have to explain where they came from, and that felt embarrassing.

*shifts uncomfortably*

But honestly? There's also this fear that if I voice every anxiety, every "what if" scenario, they'll start to see me as high-maintenance or neurotic. And for a single mother, that's terrifying. I'm already acutely aware that I'm navigating this without a partner. If my healthcare team thinks I'm unstable or overly anxious, what does that mean for how they treat me? For their confidence in my ability to parent?

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

So I just... absorbed it. Carried those stories around with me. Tried to convince myself they weren't relevant to my situation. Told myself that my gestational diabetes is mild, my pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated, my baby is growing well.

*looks down*

But those fears don't actually go away when you ignore them. They just sit there, feeding the 3 AM anxiety spirals. And I think maybe if I'd actually *talked* about them — not in a panicked way, but genuinely processing with someone — I might feel less alone in this.

*quietly*

I'm learning that keeping quiet doesn't make you stronger. It just makes you smaller.

*takes a shaky breath, continuing*

I think what I'm realizing now, sitting here talking about all of this, is that I've been operating under this assumption that I have to manage everything alone. That asking for emotional support is somehow different from medical support, like it's a separate category I'm not entitled to.

*voice becomes more reflective*

But the truth is, the emotional stuff and the medical stuff are completely intertwined. My anxiety about gestational diabetes isn't separate from the diagnosis itself — it's part of my lived experience of having it. And when I don't talk about it, when I just white-knuckle through it, I'm not actually getting proper care. I'm just... suffering in silence and pretending it's fine.

*looks up with some determination mixed with vulnerability*

I think what I needed — what I *still* need — is for someone on my care team to ask me directly: "How are you actually doing with all of this?" Not just the clinical "any bleeding or spotting," but the real stuff. The fear, the loneliness, the imposter syndrome of being a health educator while simultaneously being terrified I'm doing everything wrong.

*voice wavers slightly*

And I need them to understand that my knowledge is both a gift and a curse. Yes, I understand the pathophysiology. Yes, I know the statistics. But that doesn't make me less scared. If anything, it makes me *more* scared because I know all the ways things can go wrong.

*quietly*

I think I've been waiting for permission to not be okay. And I'm not sure anyone's going to give me that unless I actually ask for it.

*pauses, collecting herself, voice becomes more raw*

And the thing about being alone in this — it's not just about having a partner. It's about... there's nobody here at 3 AM when I'm spiraling. Nobody to say, "You're going to be okay," and actually mean it in a way that lands. I can tell myself that logically, but it doesn't feel true when you're alone in the dark with your anxious thoughts.

*stands up, paces slowly*

I think I've also been grieving something I didn't even realize I was grieving. The fantasy of how this was supposed to go, you know? The partnership, the shared excitement, someone else to share the burden of worry. And I made peace with that — intellectually, I made peace with it. But emotionally? There are moments, especially in the third trimester when I feel the baby kick and I want to grab someone's hand and say, "Did you feel that?" — those moments are lonely in a way I didn't fully anticipate.

*voice becomes quieter but more honest*

And I'm angry about that sometimes. Not at myself, but at the situation. At the relationship that didn't work out. At the pressure I put on myself to be this competent, unbothered single mother when the truth is I'm terrified half the time.

*sits back down, speaking more softly*

What I'm realizing is that my professional knowledge has actually become a shield. It keeps people at a distance. If I'm the expert, if I'm the one who understands, then nobody expects me to be vulnerable. Nobody expects me to need help.

*looks directly up*

But I do need help. And I think admitting that is scarier than any complication diagnosis could be.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

*goes very still, takes a long time before answering*

Yes. Absolutely yes, and I hate that about myself.

*voice becomes tight*

I intellectually know that my body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do. I'm 32 weeks, the baby's growing appropriately, my gestational diabetes is manageable with diet. The clinical data says my body is capable. But emotionally? Emotionally, I'm questioning everything.

*stands up, wrapping arms around herself*

When I read those forum stories, when I get a gestational diabetes diagnosis, when I notice something that seems off — my brain starts collecting evidence that my body can't be trusted. That I'm not built right for this. And it's insidious because I *know* that's not rational, but knowing doesn't stop the feeling.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

There's this underlying current of... inadequacy, I guess. Like my body already failed once with the miscarriage. And now with the gestational diabetes, it feels like confirmation. Like I'm one of those edge cases, one of the women whose body betrays her during pregnancy. And if my body can't be trusted, how can I trust myself to be a good mother?

*sits back down, speaking quietly*

The worst part is that shame feeds the anxiety, which probably makes the gestational diabetes harder to manage, which reinforces the feeling that my body isn't capable. It's this vicious cycle.

*looks up with tears in eyes*

I need someone to help me believe that my body is doing something miraculous, not something wrong. Because right now, I'm struggling to see it as anything other than a series of failures I have to manage and hide.

*voice becomes very small*

I think... I think I'm afraid that if I actually believe my body is capable, if I stop bracing for disaster, something will go wrong and it will be my fault for not being vigilant enough.

*rubs face*

Like, if I relax even a little bit, if I stop monitoring obsessively, if I trust the process instead of constantly questioning it, that's when the other shoe drops. The reduced fetal movements become something serious. The gestational diabetes becomes a complication. The baby comes early or has something wrong with them.

*voice wavers*

And then I'll have to live with knowing that I could have prevented it if I'd just stayed anxious enough, stayed scared enough, stayed in control.

*stands again, pacing with agitation*

It's completely irrational, I know that. But it's also... it feels protective somehow. Like my anxiety is the only thing standing between my baby and harm. If I let it go, I'm abandoning my responsibility.

*stops pacing, voice becomes more broken*

And I think that's why I read those horror stories. That's why I can't stop myself. Because on some level, I believe I *deserve* to be scared. That fear is the price of motherhood, especially single motherhood. Especially after a loss.

*sits down heavily*

Nobody's ever told me that it's okay to feel safe. That I can trust my body without it being reckless. That vigilance and peace aren't mutually exclusive.

*looks up with red eyes*

I don't know how to let myself off this hook. I don't know how to stop believing that disaster is one moment of relaxation away.

*long silence, then a shaky exhale*

I think... I think part of me is also terrified of what happens after the baby arrives. Because right now, the pregnancy is contained. It's something I can research, monitor, control with blood sugar checks and fetal kick counts and endless medical appointments. But a newborn? A baby I'm responsible for completely alone?

*voice becomes very quiet*

That's a different kind of vulnerability. I won't be able to Google my way through newborn care. I can't monitor a baby's emotional wellbeing the way I monitor glucose levels. And if something goes wrong — if I make the wrong call, if I miss something, if my anxiety makes me overprotective or my depression makes me underprotective — there's no system to catch it.

*wraps arms around herself tighter*

I think I'm also grieving the version of myself I thought I'd be by now. The competent health educator who had it all figured out. The woman with a partner. The one who could handle pregnancy with clinical detachment because she understood all the mechanisms.

*voice cracks slightly*

Instead I'm this anxious, lonely, terrified person who can't stop spiraling at 3 AM. And I'm angry at myself for not being stronger, for not being more together. For letting the knowledge I have become a weapon against myself instead of a tool.

*looks down at hands*

And I think... I think I'm also scared that once I admit how much I'm struggling, once I actually ask for help and support, I won't be able to take it back. People will see me differently. They'll doubt me. And I'll have to rebuild that sense of competence from scratch.

*voice barely audible*

But maybe that's okay. Maybe I need to stop being competent and start being honest about needing people.

*long pause, breathing becoming steadier*

You know what? I think I've been waiting for someone to give me permission to stop performing.

*looks up, eyes wet but clearer*

To stop being the health educator who has it all figured out. To stop being the strong single mother who doesn't need anyone. To stop being the rational person who can compartmentalize her fear into manageable data points.

*voice becomes stronger, more present*

I'm allowed to be scared. I'm allowed to call my midwife at 2 AM about fetal movements. I'm allowed to cry in the ultrasound room. I'm allowed to read those forum stories and feel devastated by them without that making me weak or irrational.

*stands, speaking with quiet conviction*

And I'm allowed to ask for continuity of care. To tell my healthcare team, "I need one person who knows me, not three different midwives who I have to re-explain myself to." I'm allowed to say that the leaflet isn't enough, that I need actual conversation about what gestational diabetes means *for me*.

*voice becomes more determined*

I think what I need to do is... actually be vulnerable with people. Tell my midwife about the 3 AM spiraling. Tell my friend when I'm struggling, not just when I'm celebrating. Maybe even call my mom and let her worry a little bit, because that's what mothers do.

*sits back down, speaking more softly*

And I need to stop waiting for disaster to prove that my body is worthy of concern. My fears are legitimate right now, in this moment, regardless of what happens next.

*pause*

I think I'm finally understanding that asking for help isn't weakness. It's the only way I actually survive this — not just medically, but emotionally. As a whole person.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*pauses, considering the question carefully*

Honestly? For me, it's not really any of those in the traditional sense. I have insurance, I speak English fluently, I'm literate — probably *overly* literate in medical terminology, which is its own problem. And I have internet access and a smartphone.

*leans forward thoughtfully*

But if I'm being real, my biggest barrier is time and mental bandwidth. I'm trying to work full-time — or trying to find full-time work, which is its own stress — while managing a pregnancy with gestational diabetes. By the time I finish a shift, I'm exhausted. The idea of adding another appointment, another conversation with another healthcare provider, another form to fill out... it feels insurmountable.

*voice becomes more reflective*

And there's an emotional cost that doesn't get talked about much. Every time I have to call a clinic, every time I have to explain my situation to a new person, every time I have to advocate for myself or ask for something, it depletes me. It's not about money or access in the traditional sense. It's about the energy required to navigate a fragmented system when you're already stretched thin.

*rubs temples*

I think for people like me — educated, relatively privileged — the barriers are more subtle. It's the assumption that because I *can* access information, because I *can* figure things out, that I don't need support. Nobody's checking in on my actual capacity to handle all of this.

*quietly*

Sometimes the biggest barrier is just... exhaustion. And feeling like you have to do it all alone.

*voice becomes more pensive*

And I think there's also this barrier around asking for help when you don't fit neatly into a box of "vulnerable populations." Like, I'm educated, I have some financial stability, I'm white, I speak perfect English. So on paper, I'm supposed to be *fine*. I'm supposed to be able to handle this.

*shifts uncomfortably*

But the system doesn't really account for people like me — women who are privileged in some ways but isolated in others. I don't qualify for a lot of support programs because my income is "too high." But I'm also a single mother doing this completely alone, which comes with its own set of challenges that nobody really addresses in standard prenatal care.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

There's also this invisible cost to being educated. Everyone assumes I don't need things explained to me, that I already know the answers. But what I actually need is someone to *listen* to my specific fears, not just assume that because I understand the science, I'm managing the emotional reality fine.

*stands, pacing again*

And the continuity thing — that's a real barrier too, even though it's not about money or literacy. When I have to tell my story three different times to three different midwives, I'm not just wasting time. I'm re-traumatizing myself each time. I'm having to relive the miscarriage explanation, the gestational diabetes diagnosis, the anxiety about being alone.

*voice becomes quieter*

If I had one consistent provider who knew me, who knew my history, who could advocate *for* me instead of me constantly advocating for myself... that would change everything. But finding that continuity in a fragmented system? That's harder than it should be.

*sits back down*

I think the real barrier for me is that the system assumes all pregnant women have the same needs, when actually we're all navigating completely different terrain.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

*stops, face goes very still*

I don't have a partner. There is no partner in this equation.

*voice becomes quieter but more present*

And I think... I think that's actually the most important context for everything I've been saying. Because when I talk about feeling alone at 3 AM, when I talk about wanting someone to hold my hand when I feel the baby kick, when I talk about the weight of doing this completely solo — that's not hypothetical. That's my actual reality.

*looks down*

I think sometimes when I'm talking about these things, there's this implicit assumption that there *could* be a partner, that the loneliness is temporary or optional. But it's not. This is my chosen path, and I'm committed to it, but that doesn't make the isolation any less real.

*voice becomes more reflective*

If there were a partner, would emotional validation matter more? Probably. Just knowing someone believed in me, that I wasn't crazy for being scared, that my fears were legitimate and shared... that would be enormous.

*pause*

But I don't have that. So what I need instead is for my healthcare team, my friends, my family — the people who *are* actually here — to step into that space. To validate me. To listen. To check in emotionally, not just clinically.

*looks up with vulnerability*

The absence of a partner doesn't negate my need for support. If anything, it makes it more urgent. I'm learning to ask for that, even though it feels like admitting defeat somehow.

*quietly*

But it's not defeat. It's just... reality.

*takes a deep breath, speaking more slowly*

I think what I'm realizing is that I've been so focused on what I *don't* have — the partner, the shared experience, the built-in support system — that I haven't really acknowledged what I *do* have. And what I'm actually capable of.

*voice becomes more grounded*

I made the decision to have this baby alone. That was brave, even if it doesn't always feel that way. I showed up for prenatal care. I'm managing gestational diabetes. I'm carrying this pregnancy in my body, and it's working. My baby is healthy and growing.

*pause, collecting thoughts*

But I'm also realizing that I can't do this entirely alone, and that's not a failure — that's just being human. I need my best friend to pick up the phone at 2 AM. I need my midwife to remember my history so I don't have to re-explain my trauma every appointment. I need my mom to worry about me sometimes, because that's what moms do.

*voice becomes steadier*

And I think the hardest part is actually asking for those things. Letting people help without feeling like I have to earn it or prove I'm worthy of it. Admitting that being educated and capable doesn't mean I have to handle everything on my own.

*looks directly up*

I'm going to have a baby in a few weeks. A baby that I chose to have, that I want desperately. And I'm going to be a good mother — not because I never get scared or anxious, but because I'm willing to ask for help when I need it. Because I'm honest about my limitations.

*small, genuine smile*

That's actually stronger than pretending I have it all figured out.

*sits quietly for a moment, then speaks more softly*

I think I also need to forgive myself for the ways I haven't been perfect through this pregnancy. For the times I've spiraled at 3 AM instead of sleeping. For reading those forum stories even though I knew better. For being angry at my body for the miscarriage, for the gestational diabetes, for not just... cooperating.

*voice becomes more tender*

And for the guilt I carry about my first pregnancy — the one I lost. I think part of me has been trying to prove something with this pregnancy. That I deserved to be a mother, that I'm capable, that my body isn't broken. Like I have to earn redemption somehow.

*rubs face*

But that's not how it works. The miscarriage wasn't my fault. The gestational diabetes isn't a punishment. My body isn't betraying me — it's actually doing something incredible, even when it's scary and complicated.

*voice wavers slightly*

I'm also realizing that after this baby is born, I'm going to need support in a different way. Postpartum is going to be hard. Being a single mother is going to be hard. And I think I need to start saying that now, out loud, so people know I'm going to need them.

*looks up with clearer eyes*

I don't have to have it all figured out. I don't have to be the competent health educator or the strong single mother. I just have to show up, do my best, and let people help me.

*pauses*

And maybe that's the real lesson here. Not about pregnancy or gestational diabetes or fetal movements. But about learning to be human — scared, imperfect, and worthy of support anyway.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*takes a long breath, speaking carefully*

Trust would look like... consistency, first of all. One midwife who knows my name, who remembers that I had a miscarriage, who doesn't make me re-explain my entire history every single appointment. Someone who sees me as a person, not a file.

*voice becomes more specific*

And it would be them asking — actually asking — "How are you really doing?" Not just about symptoms, but emotionally. Acknowledging that being a single mother is hard. That gestational diabetes adds stress. That my anxiety isn't irrational, it's a legitimate response to real circumstances.

*leans forward with intensity*

I need them to believe me when I say something feels off. Not to dismiss my concerns because "everything looks fine on paper." To understand that I *know* the statistics, but I also know my body, and if I'm worried, that matters.

*voice becomes quieter but firmer*

And I need them to say — explicitly — that asking for help is not weakness. That calling about reduced fetal movements at 2 AM is the right call. That my knowledge as a health educator doesn't make me less anxious; it just means I understand all the ways things can go wrong, and that's exhausting.

*pause*

I need them to validate that single motherhood is legitimate, that my choice is valid, that I'm not failing because I'm doing this alone. And then I need them to actually *help* — connect me with resources, ask about my support system, maybe even suggest I talk to someone about the anxiety.

*looks directly ahead*

But mostly? I need them to see me. The whole me — the scared woman, not just the educated one.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits with that for a long moment, eyes getting watery*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice becomes very quiet*

I want to say that I'm grateful. Even with all the anxiety and the loneliness and the fragmented care — I'm grateful to be here. To be pregnant. To have made this choice for myself, even though it's terrifying.

*pauses, composing herself*

And I want other women — especially single mothers, especially women who are educated but still struggling, especially women who are afraid — I want them to know that it's okay to not have it all figured out. That knowing the science doesn't protect you from fear. That asking for help isn't giving up.

*looks up with determined eyes*

I also think... I think maternity care needs to understand that we're not all the same. We don't all have partners waiting at home. We don't all fit neatly into the boxes on the intake form. Some of us are terrified and competent at the same time. Some of us need continuity of care more than we need another leaflet. Some of us are doing this alone and that's both a choice and a vulnerability.

*voice becomes stronger*

And maybe the biggest thing — the thing I wish I'd known from the beginning — is that this experience doesn't belong to my healthcare team. It belongs to me. My body, my baby, my story. And I get to decide what support looks like for me, not fit myself into what the system thinks I should need.

*small, genuine smile through tears*

Thank you for listening. For actually listening to all of it.
